coredata.dc:title: Ethanothermal synthesis of phenol-derived carbon dots with multiple color emission via a versatile oxidation strategy
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Weiwei
Meng, Xianrui
Li, Hanmei
He, Futao
Wang, Lu
Xu, Hao
Huang, Yongwei
Zhang, Wenkai
Fang, Xiaomin
Ding, Tao
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we report an ethanothermal synthesis of multicolor fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) by using a series of phenols and oxidants as the precursors. The emission colors of CDs are well-controlled at blue, green, yellow or red wavelengths by adjusting the combination of a phenol and an oxidant. And the naphthalenediol with highly reactivity and the oxidant with heavy halogen atom result in CDs exhibiting long wavelength emission. The resulting nanodots are characterized as amorphous aggregates with abundant oxygen species. The phenol-derived CDs can be used as highly processible fillers to fabricate epoxy composites, and a white light-emitting diode (LED) employing the multicolor CDs/epoxy composite as the light converter is also demonstrated.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Phenols
Photoluminescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of CDs/epoxy nanocomposites and white LEDs (WLEDs)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: AFM (a–d) and TEM images (e–h) of B-CDs (a, e), G-CDs (b, f), Y-CDs (c, g) and R-CDs (d, h). Height profiles along lines in AFM images show below. All the scale bars in TEM images represent 100 nm.
(a) The high-resolution C1s XPS spectra. (b) 1HNMR spectra. Deuterated solvents: (CD3)2SO for B-, Y- and R-CDs, and D2O for G-CDs.
(a) Extinction spectra and (b) PL emission spectra of CDs solution. (c) The PL transient decay curves of CDs excited by a 360 nm diode laser. IRF, instrument response function.

(a) Photographs of B-, G-, Y- and R-CDs/epoxy composites under irradiation from daylight (upper) and 365 nm UV lamp (lower). (b) Schematic diagram of WLEDs based on the UV LED chip coated with a mixed B-, G-, and R-CDs/epoxy composite converter layer. (c) The CIE chromaticity coordinates for the WLEDs with various applied voltages. Photograph of the WLED at 3.3 V shows inset.
Ethanothermal synthesis of multicolor CDs by using phenols and oxidants as precursors.

head.ce:title: Ethanothermal synthesis of phenol-derived carbon dots with multiple color emission via a versatile oxidation strategy

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Fine Chemistry and Engineering
Henan Engineering Laboratory of Flame-Retardant and Functional Materials
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we report an ethanothermal synthesis of multicolor fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) by using a series of phenols and oxidants as the precursors. The emission colors of CDs are well-controlled at blue, green, yellow or red wavelengths by adjusting the combination of a phenol and an oxidant. And the naphthalenediol with highly reactivity and the oxidant with heavy halogen atom result in CDs exhibiting long wavelength emission. The resulting nanodots are characterized as amorphous aggregates with abundant oxygen species. The phenol-derived CDs can be used as highly processible fillers to fabricate epoxy composites, and a white light-emitting diode (LED) employing the multicolor CDs/epoxy composite as the light converter is also demonstrated.

ce:section.ce:para: In the past decade, a new exciting class of metal-free and carbon-rich photoluminescent (PL) nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs), exhibiting distinctive advantages including high PL quantum yield (QY) [1], large-scale synthesis [2], emission color tuning [3,4], multi-element doping [5], has been developed as a promising functional component in bioimaging, sensors, photocatalysts and optoelectronic devices [6,7]. Various approaches have been developed to fabricate CDs including “top-down” and “bottom-up” methods [8,9]. The “top-down” method refers to carving bulk carbon materials into nanoparticles by using physical or chemical approaches, such as acid oxidation [10], electrochemical exfoliation [11] and hydrothermal [12]. Compared with “top-down” route, the “bottom-up” route has obvious advantages in adjusting the composition and physical properties of CDs by the careful selection of diversified organic precursors and carbonization conditions via thermal pyrolysis or carbonization [13,14]. It is proposed that crystalline CDs are generated by thermal treatment of molecular precursors consecutively through polymerization, aromatization, nucleation and growth [15–17]. Up to now, many molecular precursors with definite chemical structures are explored to synthesize CDs through bottom-up carbonization routes, such as citric acid [18,19], phenylenediamines [3], chloroform/diethylamine [4], diaminobenzenesulfonic acid [20], and trinitropyrene [21]. Nevertheless, it is still highly desirable and a great challenge to establish an extendable synthesis system for multicolor CDs covering the entire visible region, especially at long wavelength light emission.

Phenolic compounds, one of the most widely occurring groups of phytochemicals in plants, show a large diversity of structures covering from small molecules as phenol to macromolecules as flavolans [22]. In the presence of oxidants, phenols can be oxidized to generate the potential phenoxy radicals that subsequently can be polymerized to form polyphenols, which have been utilized in numerous applications such as electronic, photonic and flame retardant applications [23]. Although there were several reports of fluorescent CDs synthesis using phenolic compounds as precursors [24–27], the catalytic oxidative polymerization of various phenols have not been carried out to synthesize CDs. In this current study, we explore an extendable synthesis strategy, that is, ethanol-mediated solvothermal of phenol/oxidant system, to fabricate amorphous CDs having blue, green, yellow and red emissions. The emission colors of CDs are well-controlled by adjusting the combination of a phenol and an oxidant. Furthermore, the CDs used as highly processible fillers to fabricate polymeric composites as well as LED phosphors have also demonstrated.

Naphthalenediol (DHN), including 1,3-DHN and 2,7-DHN, o-, m-, p-dihydroxybenzene (o-, m-, p-DHB), N-bromosuccinimide (NBS), 2,3-dicyano-5,6-dichlorobenzoquinone (DDQ), anthraquinone, dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (DCC) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. K2S2O8, KIO4, methanol, ethanol, dichloromethane, N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) and other analytic reagents were purchased from the Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.

0.16 g (1 mmol) of 2,7-DHN and 0.68 g (2.5 mmol) of K2S2O8 were dissolved into 15 mL of ethanol, and transferred to a 30 ml Teflon-equipped stainless-steel autoclave. Then the solution was heated to 180 °C and kept for additional 4 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the crude product was purified via silica column chromatograph using a mixture of methanol and dichloromethane. The blue emissive part was collected and dried to obtain the viscous B-CDs. The G-, Y- and R-CDs were prepared in the same method by using m-DHB/DDQ, 1,3-DHN/K2S2O8 and 1,3-DHN/NBS as the diphenol/oxidant, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of CDs/epoxy nanocomposites and white LEDs (WLEDs)
ce:section.ce:para: To fabricate the CDs/epoxy nanocomposites, a certain amount of CDs, epoxy (CYD-128) and curing agent (D230) were mixed by vortex mixers, and then the mixture was casted into a mold and thermally cured at 100 °C for 4 h. To fabricate the WLEDs, the CDs/epoxy mixture was casted onto UV-LED chips (Led World, 365 nm, 1 W), then the coating layer was cured at 100 °C for 4 h.

The atomic force microscope (AFM) images were measured with Dimension Icon (Bruker Instruments Inc.). The transmission electron microscopy measurements were performed using a JEM1200EX at 120 kV. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) was performed on a Thermo Fisher Scientific ESCALAB 250XI photoelectron spectrometer with Al Kα (hν = 1486.6 eV) as the X-ray source. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) characterization was carried out on a Bruker Vertex 70 FTIR spectrometer. 1H NMR spectra were obtained on a Bruker AV-400 MHz spectrometer. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded with a Hitachi U4100 Spectrometer. The steady and transient PL spectra, as well as the absolute quantum yields (QYs) were performed with HORIBA FluoroLog-3 spectrophotometer.

The multicolor CDs are synthesized by solvothermal of phenol/oxidant system in ethanol media at 180 °C for 4 h in the autoclave (Scheme 1 ). The phenols include the DHN (e.g., 1,3-DHN, 2,7-DHN) and DHB (o-, m-, p-DHB). The oxidants include inorganic oxidants (e.g., K2S2O8, KIO4) and organic oxidants (e.g., DDQ, NBS). The emission colors of CDs are well-controlled at blue, green, yellow or red wavelengths by using 2,7-DHN/K2S2O8, m-DHB/DDQ, 1,3-DHN/K2S2O8 and 1,3-DHN/NBS as the combination of phenol/oxidant, respectively (Table S1). The products are weakly emissive when the solvothermal of phenol precursors are carried out in the absence of the oxidants, confirming the indispensability of the oxidants. To optimize the synthesis conditions, the effects of the phenol, oxidant, feed ratio, solvothermal media, reaction temperature and reaction time, on the emission color of the CDs, have been investigated in detail. In case of the phenol structures, the DHNs are more active than DHBs when they react with the oxidants. As the 1,3-DHN and 2,7-DHN react with K2SO8 under the same condition, the former tend to produce yellow light emission, but only blue emission is created in latter. This is because the DHN with rich electron density is more likely to be attacked by the oxidants, and hence induce more chromophores into the conjugate structures of CDs [28,29], featuring as a red-shifted emission. As an oxidant, DDQ can react with all the phenols and tends to produce strong green emissive products, while other commonly used organic oxidants, e.g., DCC and anthraquinone, can only react with the active DHN and generate weakly luminescent products. It should be noted that the halogen (Cl, Br, I) containing oxidants (DDQ, NBS and KIO4) are more likely to shift the CDs emission color to longer wavelength than the N or S containing oxidants (DCC or K2S2O8); the heavier the halogen atoms, the longer wavelength the CDs emission. Furthermore, when the feed ratio of the phenol and oxidant decreases (from 1:1 to 1:4), the emission color of the product varies from blue to long-wavelength (Table S2), indicating that the increasing oxidization level introduces the increasing number of oxygen-containing chromophores into the CDs. To estimate the role the ethanol plays in the synthesis process, DMF and water have been also used as solvothermal media (Table S3). Compared with the strong blue-emission in ethanol, a weak blue-emission in DMF and even nearly nonluminescent in water are observed in the final products, which suggest that the ethanol acts as a nucleophile regent participating in solvothermal reaction and has given an auxochromic effect on the final CDs. It has been proved that the reaction temperature plays a key role in tuning the chemical structure and PL of citric acid derived CDs [16,17]. However, the emission color and intensity of the solvothermal products from 2,7-DHN/K2S2O8 system is less dependent on the reaction temperature ranging from 180 °C to 250 °C (Table S4).

The morphology of the obtained CDs is first observed by the AFM. The methanol solution was cast on mica surface, and left the solvent volatilizing and the CDs self-assembling under room temperature. A large number of “dots” can be easily observed in all samples, although the heights and distributions have slight differences between the CDs (Fig. 1 a–d). The AFM heights of B-, G-, Y- and R-CDs reach several even dozens of nanometers. However, it is difficult to observe clear carbon lattice in these nanoparticles from the TEM (Fig. 1e–h), and only amorphous particles with dozens of nanometers in size can be observed at high sample concentration (Fig. 1g). It is predictable the carbonization of phenol has been greatly hindered in the presence of the oxidants under the solvothermal treatment. Thus, these CDs are suggested as molecular aggregates or supramolecular self-assemblies in essence [30–34].

In analogy with crystalline (graphitic) CDs, the amorphous ones have complex chemical composition, which usually contain fluorophores, oligomers and even polymers that combine together via noncovalent bondings. XPS spectra are used to characterize the chemical composition of the obtained CDs (Fig. S1). In addition to the C and O, other atoms and bonding characteristics should also be taken into account, because the oxidant K2S2O8, DDQ and NBS may introduce S, N, Cl and Br atoms into the CDs. Unexpectedly, the contents of S, N, Cl and Br are extremely low and almost negligible (Table S5), for instance, the contents of N and Cl in the m-benzenediol/DDQ derived G-CDs are only 3.9% and 2.6%, respectively. This indicates that the oxidant may not participate in the formation of carbon-heteroatom bond, only leads to the formation of various oxygen containing groups. The O/C atomic ratios in XPS full scan show significant differences in the four CDs, and find its maximum value in G-CDs, indicating a high oxidation level or large number of oxygen containing group in this sample. The high-resolution C1s XPS spectra can be fitted with three Gaussian peaks at 284.0–285.5eV (C―C/C=C), 285.5–286.5 eV (C―O) and 288–289 eV (C=O), respectively (Fig. 2 a, Table S6), and the maximum percentages of C―C/C=C and C―O/C=O are found in B-CDs and G-CDs, respectively. Thus, we can conclude that the G-CDs have richer oxygen species than another three CDs, which may provide abundant intermolecular sites to combine together to form large aggregates. The FT-IR of CDs show aromatic ring signal around 1630 cm−1, as well as abundant oxygen-containing groups, such as ―OH (3430 cm−1), C=O (1733 cm−1) and C―O (1100-1200 cm−1) (Fig. S2). 1HNMR spectra also give many clues of the chemical structure of the CDs (Fig. 2b). The characteristic signals of the aromatic hydrogen around 6–8 ppm can be found in four CDs. In addition to the aliphatic hydrogen, the -CH 2 adjacent to the ester group (at 4.0 ppm) and aromatic ether group (4.2–4.4 ppm) are observed in B-, Y- and R-CDs. Strong signals of aldehyde hydrogen (around 9.6 ppm) appear in both B-CDs and G-CDs. Based on the XPS, FTIR and 1HNMR analysis, the CDs derived from the phenol/oxidant system are suggested as the assemblies of π-conjugated heterocycles of organic molecules with various oxygen species, including carboxyl, hydroxyl, ester, ether and even aldehyde. The number and distribution of these oxygen species are vital for the molecular aggregation and even the emission color of the CDs.

The UV–vis spectra of all CDs show a strong π-π∗ absorption below 300 nm as well as a series of n-π∗ absorption above 350 nm, which correspond to various fine vibration structures at certain electronic energy level (Fig. 3 a). For example, the B-CDs show the absorption peaks at 317, 333 and 365 nm, and the Y-CDs show the absorption peaks at 457, 486 and 511 nm, respectively. The n-π∗ absorption bands redshift in the sequence of B-, G-, Y- and R-CDs, which are consistent with the shifting emission color as observed above. As shown in Fig. 3b, the maximum emission positions of B-, G-, Y- and R-CDs in methanol solutions are λem = 388, 512, 554 and 625 nm. By means of a calibrated integrating sphere method, the absolute PL quantum yields of B-, G-, Y- and R-CDs can be determined as 65.5%, 81.4%, 36.3% and 6.6%, respectively. The PL intensity decay profiles confirm the molecular PL mechanism of in CDs (Fig. 3c). The decay curves of B-, Y- and R-CDs can be fitted by a single exponential function, yielding the lifetime (τ) of 1.39 ns, 4.01 ns and 2.52 ns when detecting at 400 nm, 550 nm and 625 nm (Table S7), respectively, indicating that the CDs emissions originate from organic fluorophores. While for the G-CDs, the decay curve can be fitted by two-exponential function, yielding a short lifetime (τ 1) of 1.59 ns weighing 12% and a long lifetime (τ 2) of 10.6 ns weighing 88%, respectively.

Such CDs also exhibit excellent compatibility with various organic polymers, which enable their incorporation into polymer matrices without further chemical modifications. By virtue of the highly processibility and luminescence of CDs, the homogeneous, transparent and multicolor CDs/epoxy nanocomposites are fabricated by employing CDs as the fillers. Fig. 4 a show the optical and fluorescence images of CDs/epoxy nanocomposites. The CDs are well dispersed in epoxy matrix, giving rise to a transparency similar to neat epoxy. Furthermore, these CDs retain their fluorescent properties upon integration into epoxy matrices without any noticeable change in the emission spectra (Fig. S3), suggesting that no significant surface deterioration of the CDs occur. We have further prepared a WLED coating with a homogeneously mixed B-, G-, and R-CDs/epoxy composite layer (Fig. 4b). The emission spectrum for this device is broad and spreads over almost the entire visible range (from 400 to 700 nm, Fig. S4), and the CIE chromaticity coordinates of the WLED around (0.31, 0.33) demonstrate that the mixed CDs are capable of converting UV light into white emission (Fig. 4c).

In summary, by using phenol and oxidant as precursors, the multicolor amorphous CDs have been successfully prepared by ethanol-mediated solvothermal synthesis. The emission color of the CDs can be tuned by adjusting the combination of a phenol and an oxidant. The naphthalenediols with highly reactivity can be easily attacked by the oxidants, which result in CDs featuring as long wavelength emission. The halogen containing oxidants may shift the CDs emission color to longer wavelength than the nitrogen or sulfur containing oxidants; the heavier the halogen atoms, the longer wavelength the CDs emission. Due to the presence of oxidants, phenols are difficult to experience carbonization to form perfect carbon lattice, but tend to generate amorphous aggregates. The oxidants tend to oxidize the phenols so as to introduce abundant oxygen species such as carbonyl and carboxyl into the amorphous CDs, rather than introducing heteroatoms to form carbon-heteroatom bond. Obviously, the synthesis method described here is easily extended to a wide range of phenol/oxidant system. Using the multicolor CDs as filler, we further prepare homogeneous epoxy nanocomposites and WLEDs, making them as good phosphors in light-emitting devices.

WZ and XM performed research and wrote the paper. HL, FH and LW contributed new reagents/analytic tools; HX, YH, WZ and XF analyzed data; TD and WZ designed research. All authors read and approved the final manuscript.

This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51403051), Key Science and Technology Program of Henan Province (182102210245) and Open Research Fund of Henan Key Laboratory of Polyoxometalate Chemistry (HNPOMKF1706).
None.
The raw/processed data required to reproduce these findings cannot be shared at this time due to legal or ethical reasons.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank Guangwu Zhang for chemical structure analysis and fruitful discussion.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

The effects of synthesis conditions on the emission colors of solvothermal products, XPS analysis, PL decay curves. These materials are available in Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:para: The effects of synthesis conditions on the emission colors of solvothermal products, XPS analysis, PL decay curves. These materials are available in Supporting Information.
